preamble: The shift from the voiceless alveolar stop [t] to the postalveolar affricate [tʃ] is a primary form of palatalization. It occurs when the tongue tip is pulled toward the hard palate by a following front vowel (like [i] or [e]) or a palatal glide [j], resulting in a complex stop-fricative release.

examples.note: Allophonic palatalization where /t/ is realized as an affricate [tʃ] before /i/ or /j/ (e.g. 'chi' [tʃi]).
Historical yod-coalescence where the stop-glide sequence fused into an affricate.
Allophonic palatalization where /t/ is realized as an affricate before the high front vowel /i/.
Historical palatalization of the dental stop before a glide.
